In the previous chapters, we've covered some of the history as to how the FBI came to develop open-ended questions as a field tool and the theory behind why they work. We then began to cover some of the reasons we've refined them to become calibrated questions. In this chapter, I want to introduce you to a more in-depth look at why calibrated questions work and how to employ them effectively with your counterparts. Every negotiation begins with a state of unbelief or disbelief, which means I see things differently than you and you are wrong. When you're stuck at this stage, nothing can be accomplished because each side is unwavering in their conviction that they are right. In order to get past this, you can't just ask open-ended questions. You've got to ask open-ended questions that are calibrated to frame a direction. Once you figure out where you want the conversation to go, you've got to design the questions that will guide the conversation while letting your counterpart think it's their decision to go there. The key to getting people to see things from your point of view is a two-step process. First, don't counterpoint their ideas, but instead show your grasp of what those ideas mean to them. This is the application of tactical empathy, the showing of understanding. Tactical empathy is the precursor to all our other skills, the initiator and the booster of them. The second step is to guide them towards solving the problem. All you're doing is giving them a little nudge so they can do the work to solve their own problems. Calibrated questions make your counterpart feel like they're in charge, but it's really you who is framing the conversation. Your counterpart will have no idea how constrained they are by your question. And this is a secret to giving them the illusion of control while gaining the upper hand in a negotiation. The real beauty of calibrated questions is that they offer no target for attack like statements do. They have the power to educate your counterpart on what the problem is rather than causing conflict by telling them what the problem is. Closed into questions that drive for a yes or confrontational statements induce defensiveness. This defensiveness creates barriers at the low end of the spectrum and outright aggression at the high end because it directly intrudes on their feelings of autonomy. Lawyers love this technique. They call it cornering, which by definition overtly takes away someone's autonomy. People will die to preserve their autonomy. Do people love being confronted by lawyers? Do you? The reason calibrated questions work so well is because they give a wide range of ways to answer instead of just one word. In a nutshell, they allow you to introduce ideas and requests without sounding overbearing or pushy. When you ask a well-designed calibrated question, the implication is that you want what your counterpart wants, but you need their intelligence in order to overcome the problem. This is especially useful when you're dealing with aggressive or egotistical counterparts. You've not only asked for help without even actually asking, but you've devised a circumstance in which your counterpart, who was previously defiant, is now using their mental and emotional resources to overcome your challenges. This is a major step in getting your counterpart to internalize your way and the hurdles in it as their own. When trying to conceptualize how to best craft calibrated questions, here are a few rules to follow. Calibrated questions don't begin with words like can, is, are, do, or does. When you use one of those words, you're asking a closed-ended question, which can be answered with a simple yes or no. And in reality, most people use these to obtain a yes, which is a method that is so ridiculously overdone worldwide to have actually become counterproductive, it's self-defeating. Instead, stick to what, how, and in rare selected instances, why. Who, when, and where will usually result in your counterpart sharing a single word answer with no real effort expended thinking? Furthermore, avoid why. When you ask someone why, no matter the language, it comes across as being accusatory and it triggers defensiveness. Simply substitute what for why and you'll fix this. What are the rare instances for why when you want them to defend you? An example of this would be, why would you choose us? During a negotiation, you should employ the use of calibrated questions often and early. There are a few that you will use at the beginning of nearly every negotiation. For example, one of those questions is, what's the biggest challenge you face? Here's a list of a few questions that I like to have on standby and use in almost every negotiation. What about this is important to you? How can I help make this better for us? How would you like me to proceed? What is it that brought us into this situation? How can we solve this problem? What's the objective? What are we trying to accomplish here? How am I supposed to do that? Here's an advanced tip as well. Bundle into groups of three calibrated questions that ask essentially the same thing. Not three different things, but the same thing three different ways. I'll give you an example. You bundle these three questions. What's the biggest challenge you face? What's the key issue? What are we up against? Another example would be, how does that help? What problem does that solve? What's the objective? Remember these questions the next time that you're in a negotiation and you'll not only reveal important information, but you'll reduce your counterparts' state of unbelief and begin to make them see things from your point of view. When you use these questions, you'll get your counterpart thinking about how to solve your problems and they will get the illusion of control while you are actually leading the conversation.
